subsystem: Ceph distributed-storage messenger, legacy wire protocol (ProtocolV1). Specifically ProtocolV1::handle_connect_message_2(), the second phase of the server/accept side of the msgr v1 connect handshake. It runs after the peer's ceph_msg_connect header (connect_msg) has been received and processes it: negotiates protocol version and feature bits, enforces cephx signature/version requirements, verifies the peer's authorizer (optionally issuing a cephx challenge), then reconciles this new connection against any existing connection to the same peer address via connect_seq/global_seq bookkeeping, ultimately replacing, retrying, waiting on, resetting, or opening the session.
provenance: Identified from Ceph-specific symbols and idioms: the class/method ProtocolV1::handle_connect_message_2 (msg/async/ProtocolV1.cc), the ldout(cct, N) << ... << dendl logging macros, ceph_assert, bufferlist, AsyncConnectionRef, session_key/session_security, and the ceph_msg_connect/ceph_msg_connect_reply structures. Protocol constants such as CEPH_MSGR_TAG_BADPROTOVER, CEPH_MSGR_TAG_BADAUTHORIZER, CEPH_MSGR_TAG_CHALLENGE_AUTHORIZER, CEPH_AUTH_CEPHX, CEPH_ENTITY_TYPE_OSD/MDS/MGR, CEPH_FEATURE_MSG_AUTH, CEPH_FEATUREMASK_CEPHX_V2 and HAVE_FEATURE(...,CEPHX_V2) are all Ceph messenger identifiers. The banner comment 'sample: primevul_train_2406_fixed' marks this as the patched revision of a PrimeVul sample; the corpus filename encodes CWE-294 (Authentication Bypass by Capture-replay).
trust_boundary: This is the accepting (server) side of a network handshake, so the entire connect_msg (a ceph_msg_connect the peer sent over the socket) is attacker-controlled: connect_seq, global_seq, host_type, features, protocol_version, and authorizer_protocol are all hostile wire values, as is the authorizer payload in authorizer_buf/auth_bl_copy that ms_deliver_verify_authorizer consumes. A remote or man-in-the-middle attacker fully controls these fields and can replay bytes captured from a legitimate session. Trusted inputs are the local CephContext config (cct->_conf->cephx_*), the messenger policy tables, and this daemon's own state; the authorizer verification callback is the boundary that actually authenticates the peer — everything the code does with peer_type/policy BEFORE that callback returns success is done on unauthenticated, spoofable data.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The method takes no explicit parameters; its inputs are member state, chiefly connect_msg (the ceph_msg_connect header the peer sent) and connection (the AsyncConnection being negotiated). It first logs the peer-supplied connect_seq and global_seq at high debug verbosity. It then immediately applies two peer-controlled values: connection->set_peer_type(connect_msg.host_type) records the entity type the peer CLAIMS to be (OSD/MON/MDS/MGR/client), and messenger->get_policy(connect_msg.host_type) selects the Policy (lossy/server/standby/resetcheck flags and required/supported feature masks) for that claimed type. The subsequent ldout dumps the chosen policy flags and the peer's advertised feature bitmap (connect_msg.features). Crucially all of this happens before the authorizer is verified, so the identity used to pick the policy is still unauthenticated.

A ceph_msg_connect_reply is stack-allocated and zeroed with memset (the comment disclaims security relevance; it is standard struct initialization before filling fields for the wire reply). An empty authorizer_reply bufferlist is prepared to carry any auth response bytes back to the peer. reply.protocol_version is set to this daemon's expected msgr v1 protocol version for the (now peer-type-dependent) connection->peer_type. The log line prints our version and the peer's claimed connect_msg.protocol_version so a mismatch is diagnosable.

If the peer's protocol_version does not equal ours, the handshake is aborted early by sending a CEPH_MSGR_TAG_BADPROTOVER reply. This is the version gate; it does not authenticate anything, it only ensures both sides speak the same msgr v1 revision before more work is done.

When the peer selected cephx (connect_msg.authorizer_protocol == CEPH_AUTH_CEPHX) the server conditionally escalates the required feature mask. The branch chosen depends on connection->peer_type, which was set from the peer-supplied host_type at line 9: cluster daemons (OSD/MDS/MGR) consult cephx_require_signatures / cephx_cluster_require_signatures and cephx_(cluster_)require_version, whereas everything else (the else branch, i.e. clients/services) consults the service_* variants. When configured, it ORs CEPH_FEATURE_MSG_AUTH (message signing) and/or CEPH_FEATUREMASK_CEPHX_V2 (the cephx v2 challenge protocol) into connection->policy.features_required. These required bits are how the server can compel a peer to use message authentication and cephx v2; note the enforcement is entirely config-gated — if the operator has not set the require_* options, no bits are added and neither signing nor cephx v2 is mandatory.

feat_missing computes the bits the policy requires but the peer did not advertise: features_required AND NOT(connect_msg.features). If any required bit is missing, the connection is rejected with CEPH_MSGR_TAG_FEATURES. This is the sole place feature requirements are enforced; if features_required is empty (because the cephx v2 / MSG_AUTH require_* config was off in the block above), feat_missing is zero regardless of what the peer offers, so a peer advertising no security features passes.

The peer's authorizer bytes (authorizer_buf) are copied into a local bufferlist auth_bl_copy, then connection->lock is released so the potentially blocking verification callback can run without holding the connection mutex. Two booleans set up the cephx-v2 anti-replay challenge: need_challenge is HAVE_FEATURE(connect_msg.features, CEPHX_V2) — i.e. the server decides to demand a challenge ONLY IF the PEER advertised the CEPHX_V2 feature bit in its own connect_msg.features — and had_challenge records whether an authorizer_challenge nonce already exists on this connection (i.e. we are on the second round-trip after having already sent a challenge).

This is the authentication step. ms_deliver_verify_authorizer validates the peer's authorizer via the registered auth handler, filling authorizer_valid, session_key, authorizer_reply, and — only when need_challenge is true — a fresh server nonce into authorizer_challenge (passed by address only in that case). If verification fails OR authorizer_valid is false, the lock is retaken and state is re-checked (it may have been mark_down'd during the unlocked window; if so it must be CLOSED and we _fault()). Then the branch at line 112 implements the cephx v2 challenge: if a challenge was demanded (need_challenge), none had been sent yet (!had_challenge), and the handler produced a nonce (authorizer_challenge), the server replies CEPH_MSGR_TAG_CHALLENGE_AUTHORIZER forcing the peer to re-sign the authorizer over the server nonce — this is the capture-replay defense. Otherwise the authorizer is simply rejected with CEPH_MSGR_TAG_BADAUTHORIZER and session_security is cleared. Falling through past this block (no early return) means the authorizer verified successfully.

Having authenticated, the code looks up any pre-existing connection to the same peer address set (messenger->lookup_conn(*connection->peer_addrs)). inject_delay() is a test/fault-injection hook that can sleep here to widen races deliberately. The connection lock (released at line 89) is re-acquired and state is re-validated: while unlocked the connection could have been marked down concurrently, so if state is no longer ACCEPTING_WAIT_CONNECT_MSG_AUTH it must be CLOSED and the handshake faults out. This is the TOCTOU guard around the unlocked verification window.

The existing-connection reconciliation begins. A lookup that returned this same connection is discarded (existing = nullptr). An existing connection speaking a different protocol version (proto_type != 1) is marked down and dropped. If a genuine existing ProtocolV1 connection remains, its lock is taken as a SECOND AsyncConnection lock — the comment explicitly notes lockdep is skipped because two connection mutexes are held simultaneously, relying on an ordering assumption ('existing will not acquire this connection's lock'). The existing protocol object is down-cast to ProtocolV1* and asserted non-null and proto_type==1.

This block resolves several cases against the existing connection. If it is already CLOSED, drop it and open() a fresh session. If it is mid-replacement (exproto->replacing), a racing replace is in progress, so tell the peer RETRY_GLOBAL with the existing peer_global_seq. If the peer's connect_msg.global_seq is older than exproto->peer_global_seq the connect is stale and the server responds RETRY_GLOBAL to force the peer to bump its global sequence; otherwise the global_seq 'looks ok'. If the existing channel is lossy, it is unconditionally reset and replaced. These global_seq comparisons are part of the anti-replay/session-continuity machinery: monotonic sequence numbers are meant to prevent an attacker from re-establishing or hijacking a session with stale captured values.

This is the connect_seq reconciliation, the heart of session-race and reset handling. connect_seq == 0 while the existing side has a non-zero connect_seq is treated as a hard reset from the peer: is_reset_from_peer is set, and if the policy does resetcheck the existing session is reset before replace(). A peer connect_seq lower than the existing one is a stale/old attempt, answered with RETRY_SESSION carrying existing connect_seq+1. Equal connect_seq means either the peer should have bumped (existing already OPENED/STANDBY → RETRY_SESSION, unless both are zero under resetcheck, in which case replace to avoid deadlock) or a genuine simultaneous-connect race, broken deterministically by comparing legacy addresses (or existing->policy.server): the lower address / server side lets the incoming connection win via replace(), otherwise the server tells the peer to WAIT and keepalives the existing connection. All of these decisions are driven by attacker-supplied connect_seq/global_seq values.

The remaining existing-connection case: two asserts encode the invariant that we only reach here when the peer's connect_seq strictly exceeds the existing one and global_seq is not older — these are hard aborts if violated, so a peer that could arrange a state where the asserts fail would crash the daemon. If policy.resetcheck holds and the existing connect_seq is 0, the server tells the peer to RESETSESSION; otherwise this is a normal reconnect and replace() is called. When there is no existing connection: if this side previously reset but the peer still presents connect_seq > 0 (and we are not already replacing), send RESETSESSION; otherwise it is a brand-new session and open() proceeds. The function's return type CtPtr is a continuation pointer selecting the next handshake state.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the CWE-294 (capture-replay) crux. Whether the server issues a cephx-v2 server nonce challenge is decided by need_challenge = HAVE_FEATURE(connect_msg.features, CEPHX_V2) — a bit the PEER sets in its own connect_msg. Because the challenge address is passed to ms_deliver_verify_authorizer only when need_challenge is true, an attacker who captured a valid authorizer from a legitimate session (e.g. a passive on-path observer of msgr v1 traffic, which is unencrypted) can replay those exact bytes while simply NOT advertising the CEPHX_V2 feature. Then need_challenge is false, no fresh nonce is folded into the verification, the replayed authorizer verifies, and the peer is authenticated without proving possession of the session secret — a full authentication bypass by replay. This is the exact weakness fixed by CVE-2018-1128: the challenge/nonce must be REQUIRED, not left to the peer's opt-in. The implementation is safe only if the server can force cephx v2 (via features_required at lines 51-57/68-74) so that a peer omitting CEPHX_V2 is rejected at the feat_missing gate before ever reaching verification — which requires the operator to have enabled cephx_require_version>=2. Absent that config, the replay path at line 118 (CEPH_MSGR_TAG_BADAUTHORIZER) is only reached on genuinely invalid authorizers, not replayed-valid ones.

The peer's claimed host_type is trusted to select both the security policy and, at lines 40-42, WHICH cephx require_* configuration applies (cluster OSD/MDS/MGR path vs the service/client else-branch). Because this happens before the authorizer is verified, an attacker chooses which enforcement branch runs by simply lying about host_type. If cephx_cluster_require_signatures is set but cephx_service_require_signatures is not (a common asymmetry), a peer claiming a non-cluster host_type routes into the else branch and escapes the MSG_AUTH/cephx-v2 requirement, weakening or disabling message signing and the replay challenge for that session. The policy (lossy/server/resetcheck) chosen here also steers the whole connect_seq reconciliation below. The implementation is safe only if the authorizer verification independently binds and validates the real entity type and the require_* posture cannot be downgraded by a spoofed host_type; otherwise this is an authentication-bypass-by-spoofing lever (CWE-290) that directly amplifies the replay issue above.

The only mechanism that forces a peer to use the replay-resistant cephx v2 challenge is this config-gated OR into features_required, later enforced by the feat_missing check at lines 79-86. This is a negotiation-downgrade hazard (CWE-757): if neither cephx_require_version nor cephx_(cluster|service)_require_version is >= 2 — the default on many deployments and the whole reason CVE-2018-1128 was exploitable — CEPH_FEATUREMASK_CEPHX_V2 is never added, feat_missing stays zero for a v2-less peer, and the need_challenge branch above is never taken because the attacker withheld the CEPHX_V2 feature bit anyway. The security of the entire handshake therefore rests on an operator opt-in rather than a secure default; the code should be audited to confirm the shipped default now requires v2 and that a peer cannot negotiate the protection away.

connection->lock is dropped at line 89 and only retaken at line 103, so the verification callback runs with the connection unlocked. This is a deliberate TOCTOU window: state, connect_msg-derived fields, and authorizer_challenge can be mutated by another thread (a concurrent mark_down, a racing replace, or a second connect attempt) while unlocked. The code mitigates it by re-checking state == ACCEPTING_WAIT_CONNECT_MSG_AUTH after re-locking (lines 104-110 and again 136-142) and asserting state == CLOSED otherwise. The failure mode if that guard is incomplete is a use-after-state-change or acting on the connection after it was torn down; the invariant to confirm is that EVERY early-return path after line 89 either re-acquires the lock and re-validates state first, or is unreachable — and that authorizer_challenge/session_key writes performed under the callback are safe against concurrent readers. The ceph_assert(state == CLOSED) also means a race that leaves state in some third value aborts the process (DoS).

Two AsyncConnection locks are held at once and lockdep is deliberately bypassed, with correctness resting entirely on the comment's assumption that 'existing will not acquire this connection's lock.' If that ordering assumption is ever violated by another code path (two peers racing to connect to each other, so each accept path locks the other's connection), the daemon deadlocks (CWE-833 lock-order inversion), a remotely-influenceable denial of service since the peer controls whether a matching existing connection is present via connect_msg addressing/seq. Additionally, dynamic_cast to ProtocolV1* followed by ceph_assert(exproto) turns any unexpected protocol object into a process abort rather than a graceful error. Confirm the global lock-ordering discipline that makes the skip-lockdep safe, and that existing can never be freed between lookup_conn (line 131) and this lock (the AsyncConnectionRef refcount should hold it, but verify existing->mark_down at line 151 does not drop the last ref before use).

The connect_seq/global_seq reconciliation is the anti-replay/session-continuity logic, and every comparison operand from the peer (connect_msg.connect_seq, connect_msg.global_seq) is attacker-controlled. Once authentication is (mis)passed — see the replay finding above — an attacker who can present or replay a valid authorizer can drive these values to force session RESET/REPLACE outcomes: e.g. sending connect_seq==0 against an existing non-zero session triggers is_reset_from_peer and (under resetcheck) exproto->session_reset(), tearing down the peer's live session and its queued messages, a targeted denial of service or a way to knock a victim off and take over the session slot. The asserts at lines 291-292 (connect_seq > exproto->connect_seq, global_seq >= peer_global_seq) also become remotely-triggerable aborts if the preceding branch logic can be steered to reach them with values that violate the assertion. The invariant to confirm is that sequence numbers are only honored AFTER the authorizer is cryptographically verified for THIS session (not a replayed one) and that no attacker-reachable branch can reach the asserts with a violating (connect_seq, global_seq) pair.

summary: This is Ceph msgr v1 ProtocolV1::handle_connect_message_2, the server-side connect handshake that authenticates a fully attacker-controlled ceph_msg_connect and reconciles it against existing sessions. The dominant risk is CWE-294 capture-replay: the cephx v2 anti-replay challenge is issued only when the PEER advertises the CEPHX_V2 feature (need_challenge at line 95), so unless the operator has configured cephx_require_version>=2 to force that bit via features_required, a captured/replayed authorizer authenticates without a nonce — the exact CVE-2018-1128 weakness. Secondary risks are pre-authentication trust of the spoofable connect_msg.host_type to pick the enforcement policy (downgrade lever), a deliberate lock-drop TOCTOU window and a lockdep-bypassed double-connection lock, and attacker-driven connect_seq/global_seq handling that can force session resets and reach remotely-triggerable asserts.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Accept-side handshake handler consuming attacker-controlled connect_msg; overall authentication surface (see replay finding).
Attacker-controlled sequence number driving session reset/replace decisions (CWE-294 / DoS finding).
Attacker-controlled sequence number gating RETRY_GLOBAL and asserted invariants.
Peer-claimed identity used pre-auth to select policy and require_* branch (CWE-290 finding).
Attacker-controlled feature bitmap; CEPHX_V2 bit decides whether the replay challenge is issued.
Simple equality gate against local version; no buffer/auth contract, only rejects mismatched revisions.
Selects the auth handler; the security contract lives in ms_deliver_verify_authorizer, covered by the replay finding.
Stack struct zero-init before filling reply fields; no attacker input, no overflow.
Local value derived from our own config/peer_type; no untrusted length or copy.
Config-gated signing requirement; behavior folded into the host_type downgrade and negotiation findings.
Only mechanism forcing replay-resistant cephx v2; downgrade if not configured (CWE-757 finding).
Correct required-vs-offered check; its effectiveness depends entirely on features_required being populated, covered by the negotiation finding.
bufferlist copy of the authorizer bytes; length-safe container, no fixed buffer; content risk is the replay finding.
Deliberate unlocked verification window; TOCTOU/state-race finding.
Peer opt-in decides whether the anti-replay nonce is required — the central CWE-294 anchor.
Authentication call; challenge passed only when need_challenge — replay bypass.
Correct once-guard for the challenge round-trip; the defect is that it is only reached when the peer opted into v2, covered by the replay finding.
TOCTOU re-validation guard; noted within the lock-drop finding, correct as written.
Refcounted lookup; lifetime concern folded into the double-lock finding.
Test-only fault-injection hook; no production security contract beyond widening the race noted in the lock-drop finding.
Second-connection lock with lockdep bypassed; lock-order-inversion/deadlock finding (CWE-833).
Down-cast guarded by assert; abort-on-unexpected-type is a robustness note folded into the double-lock finding.
Remotely-influenceable assertions that abort the process if branch logic can reach them with violating seq values; part of the seq-handling finding.
Attacker connect_seq==0 tears down an existing session; DoS/session-takeover, part of seq-handling finding.
Enumerated protocol reply constants and the reply dispatcher; control-flow signaling with no independent memory/auth contract.

findings.title: Cephx authorizer capture-replay: server nonce challenge is peer-opt-in
findings.anchor: bool need_challenge = HAVE_FEATURE(connect_msg.features, CEPHX_V2); ... need_challenge ? &authorizer_challenge : nullptr
findings.bug_class: Authentication bypass by capture-replay (missing server nonce)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (ms_deliver_verify_authorizer / cephx handler; file not provided) plus this handshake site
findings.invariant: The server MUST fold a fresh, server-chosen nonce (the cephx v2 challenge) into every authorizer verification and MUST reject peers that cannot complete the challenge; whether a challenge is required must NOT be selectable by the untrusted peer's advertised feature bits.
findings.failure_mode: msgr v1 is unencrypted, so an on-path attacker can capture a legitimate peer's authorizer bytes. Replaying those bytes while omitting the CEPHX_V2 feature bit makes need_challenge false, so no nonce is passed to ms_deliver_verify_authorizer; the stale authorizer verifies and the attacker is authenticated without possessing the session secret. Unless features_required forces CEPH_FEATUREMASK_CEPHX_V2 (config cephx_require_version>=2), the feat_missing gate never rejects the v2-less peer, so the replay path is open.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the effective default (and any un-overridable enforcement) requires cephx v2 so a peer omitting CEPHX_V2 is rejected at feat_missing BEFORE verification, and that ms_deliver_verify_authorizer refuses authorizers when no challenge was performed.

findings.title: Pre-authentication trust of spoofable connect_msg.host_type selects the security policy and require_* branch
findings.bug_class: Authentication bypass by spoofing / security-decision on untrusted input
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0180_cwe294.cpp (with policy tables in implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: Security posture (which cephx signature/version requirements apply, lossy/server/resetcheck policy) must be derived from an AUTHENTICATED identity, not from the peer's self-declared host_type used before the authorizer is verified.
findings.failure_mode: The peer freely sets host_type. At lines 40-42 this chooses the cluster (OSD/MDS/MGR) vs service/client enforcement branch. A deployment that requires signatures/cephx-v2 only on the cluster path can be downgraded by a peer claiming a non-cluster host_type, dropping into the else branch and escaping CEPH_FEATURE_MSG_AUTH / CEPH_FEATUREMASK_CEPHX_V2 requirements — directly enabling the replay finding above. The chosen policy also steers the entire connect_seq reconciliation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the authorizer verification independently binds and enforces the real entity type/require_* posture so a spoofed host_type cannot select a weaker enforcement branch.

findings.title: Cephx v2 enforcement is config-gated and negotiable-away (secure-default / downgrade)
findings.bug_class: Downgrade / selection of less-secure protocol during negotiation
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0180_cwe294.cpp (defaults in implementation/config, file not provided)
findings.invariant: The replay-resistant cephx v2 challenge must be required by a secure default that the peer cannot negotiate away; the require bit must be added to features_required whenever cephx is in use, not only when an operator opted in.
findings.failure_mode: If neither cephx_require_version nor cephx_(cluster|service)_require_version is >= 2, CEPH_FEATUREMASK_CEPHX_V2 is never ORed into features_required, so feat_missing (lines 79-86) is zero for a peer that advertises no v2 support, and the need_challenge branch is skipped because that same peer withheld CEPHX_V2 — leaving the capture-replay path fully open. Protection depends entirely on operator configuration.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the shipped default requires cephx v2 (require_version defaults to >=2) and that no peer-supplied value can clear CEPH_FEATUREMASK_CEPHX_V2 from features_required before the feat_missing check.

findings.title: Attacker-driven connect_seq/global_seq can force session resets and reach remotely-triggerable asserts
findings.bug_class: Session reset / replay-driven denial of service and reachable assertion abort
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0180_cwe294.cpp (session_reset/replace in implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: Sequence-number-driven reset/replace decisions must act only on an authenticator proven fresh for THIS session, and no attacker-reachable branch may reach the connect_seq/global_seq asserts (lines 291-292) with values that violate them.
findings.failure_mode: Once an attacker gets past authentication (see replay finding) or replays a valid authorizer, sending connect_seq==0 against a live existing session sets is_reset_from_peer and (under resetcheck) calls exproto->session_reset(), destroying the victim's queued messages and connection — a targeted DoS or session-slot takeover. If branch logic can be steered to reach ceph_assert(connect_msg.connect_seq > exproto->connect_seq) with an equal/lower value, the process aborts (remote DoS).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm reset/replace only follow verified-fresh authentication and that the asserts at lines 291-292 are unreachable for any attacker-controllable (connect_seq, global_seq) pair (convert to graceful rejection if not).

findings.title: Unlocked authorizer-verification window (TOCTOU) with assert-on-unexpected-state
findings.anchor: connection->lock.unlock(); ... ms_deliver_verify_authorizer(...) ... connection->lock.lock(); if (state != ACCEPTING_WAIT_CONNECT_MSG_AUTH) { ceph_assert(state == CLOSED); return _fault(); }
findings.bug_class: Time-of-check/time-of-use race across dropped lock; assert-driven abort
findings.invariant: Every path that resumes after the lock is dropped at line 89 must re-acquire the lock and re-validate state before acting on the connection, and concurrent writes to authorizer_challenge/session_key/state must be safe; a race must not leave state in a value other than the expected ones.
findings.failure_mode: During the unlocked window another thread can mark_down or replace the connection. The guards re-check state and _fault() when it changed, but they ceph_assert(state == CLOSED); a concurrent transition to any other state aborts the daemon (DoS). If any post-line-89 return path skipped the re-lock/re-check it would act on torn-down state.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm all returns after line 89 re-lock and re-validate state, that state can only be ACCEPTING_WAIT_CONNECT_MSG_AUTH or CLOSED at the guard, and that authorizer_challenge writes are race-safe.

findings.title: Two AsyncConnection locks held with lockdep bypassed (lock-order inversion / deadlock)
findings.anchor: existing->lock.lock();  // skip lockdep check (we are locking a second AsyncConnection here)
findings.invariant: When two connection mutexes are held simultaneously a global consistent lock ordering must guarantee the reverse acquisition never occurs; the existing AsyncConnectionRef must stay alive for the duration of use.
findings.failure_mode: Lockdep is explicitly disabled here, so the safety rests only on the comment's assumption. Two peers racing to connect to each other could each take the other's connection lock in the opposite order and deadlock both accept threads — a remotely-influenceable DoS since the peer controls whether a matching existing connection exists. dynamic_cast+ceph_assert also aborts on any unexpected protocol object.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the documented lock ordering makes the second acquisition always safe (no reverse path) and that existing cannot be freed between lookup_conn (line 131), mark_down (line 151), and this lock.

audit_checklist: Verify cephx v2 (CEPH_FEATUREMASK_CEPHX_V2) is required by secure DEFAULT so a peer omitting the CEPHX_V2 feature is rejected at the feat_missing gate before ms_deliver_verify_authorizer — closing the capture-replay path (CVE-2018-1128).
Confirm ms_deliver_verify_authorizer rejects authorizers that were not verified against a fresh server challenge/nonce, i.e. that need_challenge==false cannot accept a replayed authorizer.
Confirm the effective entity type and cephx require_* enforcement are bound to the AUTHENTICATED identity, not to the pre-auth peer-supplied connect_msg.host_type (lines 9-10, 40-42).

Check that no attacker-reachable branch can reach ceph_assert(connect_msg.connect_seq > exproto->connect_seq) / ceph_assert(global_seq >= peer_global_seq) (lines 291-292) with violating values — otherwise a remote abort.
Confirm connect_seq==0 hard-reset handling (lines 217-229) cannot be used by a replaying/spoofing peer to reset a victim's live session (session_reset destroys out_queue).

Confirm every early-return after connection->lock.unlock() (line 89) re-acquires the lock and re-validates state, and that state at the guard can only be ACCEPTING_WAIT_CONNECT_MSG_AUTH or CLOSED (no third value that aborts).

Confirm the global lock ordering that justifies skipping lockdep when locking existing->lock (line 158) is sound in both directions, and that the existing AsyncConnectionRef holds a live reference through mark_down and use.
Confirm authorizer_challenge / session_key writes performed under the unlocked verification callback are safe against concurrent access.
